narrative:
Bryan Steil is Paul Ryan's understudy who inherited the master's congressional seat and his gift for making corporate-friendly policy sound like populism. A former Ryan aide who won the retiring Speaker's endorsement in 2018, Steil now chairs the House Administration Committee, where he oversees the very election rules that determine who gets to vote and how. It is a fox-guarding-the-henhouse arrangement that would make his mentor proud.

To his credit, Steil introduced the Stop Insider Trading Act in January 2026 to ban members of Congress, their spouses, and dependent children from purchasing individual stocks. The bill cleared committee on a party-line vote and awaits a full House vote. On paper, this aligns with one of the 80% Bill's most popular planks. But the devil lurks in the details: the bill only bans new purchases while allowing existing holdings to be sold on a seven-day public notice, a loophole critics argue still gives insiders a timing advantage. Democrats on the committee called it "not a stock trading ban" at all.

Meanwhile, Steil's campaign coffers read like a Wall Street Christmas card list. His $5.3 million 2024 war chest was stocked with maximum donations from the PACs of Allstate, Comcast, Morgan Stanley, Prudential, Amazon, JP Morgan, Wells Fargo, Charter Communications, and State Farm. Billionaire Republican donor Diane Hendricks of ABC Supply and Epic Systems CEO Judith Faulkner also wrote maximum checks. When every major bank and insurance company in America is funding your re-election, your stock trading ban starts to look less like reform and more like reputation laundering.

Then there is the ACE Act, Steil's crown jewel election reform bill, which Democracy Docket called "the most restrictive elections bill in decades." Branded as "election integrity," it bans automatic mail-in ballots, ends ranked choice voting, restricts ballot collection, loosens coordination rules between party committees and candidates, and raises contribution limits for political parties. In other words, Steil's idea of fair elections is one where it is harder to vote and easier to donate.

On the 80% Bill's other planks, Steil is conspicuously absent. No action on lobbying bans, term limits, Pentagon audits, paid family leave, dark money disclosure, or banning corporate PACs. He voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that extended Trump-era tax cuts benefiting the ultra-wealthy while gutting Medicaid and food assistance. His "election integrity" crusade conveniently ignores the tidal wave of corporate cash flooding his own campaigns. Steil has mastered the Ryan playbook: talk like a reformer, legislate like a corporatist, and trust that the voters back in Janesville will not read the fine print.

W. Gregory Steube is a Republican representing Florida's 17th Congressional District, which spans the Sarasota area and the rural interior of southwest Florida. A U.S. Army veteran who served as a JAG Corps officer and Chief of Detainee Operations in Iraq with the 25th Infantry Division [1], Steube parlayed his military credentials and a stint in the Florida legislature into a House seat in 2018. He now sits on the Energy and Commerce, Transportation and Infrastructure, and Science, Space, and Technology committees [2]. In January 2023, he fell twenty-five feet from a ladder while cutting trees on his property, fracturing his pelvis and puncturing a lung [3] -- a brush with mortality that did not, as far as anyone can tell, produce any corresponding brush with legislative humility.

On the twenty planks of the 80% Bill, Steube is a man of selective conviction. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, committing to cosponsor a constitutional amendment limiting House members to three terms and senators to two [4] -- a painless promise from a congressman in a deep-red district who can serve as long as he likes. He also voted in 2021 to repeal the outdated 2002 Iraq AUMF, citing Congress's constitutional war-making authority [5], a position that aligns him with the bipartisan supermajority of Americans who want Congress to reclaim that power. Credit where due: an Army veteran voting to end a blank-check war authorization carries a credibility that most of his colleagues cannot claim.

That is roughly where the populism ends. Steube has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, audit the Pentagon, ban dark money, or enact the DISCLOSE Act. He has taken no action on banning corporate PACs, closing the carried interest loophole, or establishing paid family leave. On Medicare drug price negotiation, he voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its landmark provision empowering Medicare to negotiate directly with pharmaceutical companies [6] -- a provision supported by nearly ninety percent of voters. He has shown no interest in banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, enacting police body camera mandates, banning junk fees, or advancing right-to-repair legislation. His Heritage Action score of 94% [7] tells you most of what you need to know about which side of the populist divide he lands on when the votes actually matter.

Follow the money and the pattern crystallizes. Steube's campaigns have drawn heavily from real estate interests, the insurance industry, and health sector PACs [8]. The NRA has been a consistent booster -- he has sponsored more pro-gun bills than any member of the Florida legislature during his time in Tallahassee [9] -- and his legislative energy flows reliably toward the culture-war issues that keep the base energized without threatening anyone's bottom line, including his Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act [10]. Meanwhile, the structural reforms that would curb the influence of the very donors funding his campaigns remain conspicuously absent from his agenda.

Steube is, in the final analysis, a congressman who has seen war up close and still votes to let the Pentagon spend without audit, who signed a term limits pledge while opposing virtually every reform that would make elections fairer, and who champions the Second Amendment with the fervor of a man whose donors expect nothing less. On the 80% Bill's twenty issues, he lands on the popular side perhaps twice -- and spends the rest of his energy making sure the other eighteen never come to a vote.

Haley Stevens got her start in politics the way most people get their start in manufacturing policy: by watching an entire industry nearly die and then being handed a clipboard. As Chief of Staff to Obama's Auto Rescue Task Force during the Great Recession, Stevens helped coordinate the federal intervention that saved General Motors, Chrysler, and roughly 200,000 Michigan jobs — an experience that apparently convinced her that government can do useful things when it stops arguing about whether it should [1]. She parlayed that credential into a 2018 win in Michigan's 11th District, flipping a Republican-held seat in Oakland County and bringing to Congress the rare perspective of someone who has actually set foot on a factory floor.

Stevens has built her legislative identity around the intersection of manufacturing, STEM education, and the radical notion that America should make things. Her first bill signed into law, the Building Blocks of STEM Act, directed the National Science Foundation to expand research opportunities for girls and students of color in science and technology — a measure so commonsensical that it passed with bipartisan support, which in Washington qualifies as a minor miracle [2]. She followed that up by launching the first-ever Congressional Women in STEM Caucus in 2020, co-chaired with a Republican colleague, because apparently someone had to point out that half the population might have something to contribute to rocket science [3].

Her crown jewel, legislatively speaking, is the CHIPS and Science Act, in which Stevens played a central drafting role as chair of the House Research and Technology Subcommittee. The bill included multiple provisions she authored — the CHIPPING IN Act, the NIST for the Future Act, and expansions to the Manufacturing Extension Partnership program — and has since catalyzed nearly $400 billion in domestic semiconductor investment [4]. The Alliance for Automotive Innovation handed her a 2024 Leadership Award for her work ensuring that auto-grade chips would be part of the package, a distinction that carries particular weight in a district where "supply chain disruption" is not an abstraction but a reason your neighbor got laid off [5].

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Stevens checks a reliable number of boxes. She supports Medicare drug-price negotiation, paid family leave, union protections, and closing corporate tax loopholes — positions consistent with a lawmaker who co-chairs the bipartisan House Manufacturing Caucus and whose donor list includes both organized labor endorsements and a $39,000 contribution from Amazon and Blue Origin PACs, a combination that requires a certain rhetorical flexibility [6][7]. Her near-perfect attendance record — missing just 7 of 3,642 votes through early 2026 — suggests she takes the "showing up" part of the job description literally, even if her 2025 announcement of a Senate run to replace retiring Gary Peters hints that she has begun to find the House chamber a bit small for her ambitions [8].

Stevens represents the wing of the Democratic Party that believes you can be pro-union and pro-innovation simultaneously, that industrial policy is not a contradiction in terms, and that the best argument for government competence is occasionally demonstrating some. In a Congress where manufacturing policy often takes a back seat to whatever is trending on social media, she remains stubbornly focused on the un-glamorous work of keeping American factories competitive — a priority that polls extremely well and legislates extremely slowly, which may be the most concise summary of the 80% Bill's entire thesis.

Kim Schrier wants you to know she's a pediatrician. She mentions it roughly as often as she checks your kid's blood pressure -- which is to say, constantly. The first pediatrician ever elected to Congress, Schrier flipped Washington's 8th District in 2018 and has held it through sheer force of "I'm a doctor, not a politician" branding. It's a compelling pitch, right up until you look at the fine print.

To her credit, Schrier pledged to reject corporate PAC money back in 2018, and she's actually one of only four Democrats on the powerful Energy and Commerce Committee who didn't take donations from the top pharmaceutical PACs during the 2024 cycle -- a club so exclusive it includes Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, which is the bare minimum you'd expect from someone who spent twenty years watching families choose between prescriptions and groceries.

But here's where Doctor Schrier's chart gets complicated. She co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading -- noble stuff -- then her husband quietly purchased up to $1 million in Apple stock without bothering to disclose it for three and a half months, blowing past the STOCK Act's 45-day deadline like it was a suggested speed limit. Her office explained she was "unaware" of the transaction, which is either a damning indictment of spousal communication or a convenient case of legislative amnesia. You can't champion transparency while your household portfolio is playing hide-and-seek with federal disclosure laws.

Schrier sits on Energy and Commerce, one of the most lobbied committees in Congress, overseeing health care, telecom, and energy policy. She talks a good game on lowering drug prices and expanding access, but the committee itself is a revolving door of industry influence. Her top donor industries include health professionals and the insurance sector -- the very people whose prices she promises to lower. It's the congressional equivalent of a doctor prescribing medicine manufactured by her biggest campaign supporters.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Schrier checks some boxes: she supports Medicare drug negotiation and has backed campaign finance reforms. But she's been conspicuously quiet on Pentagon audits, the lobbying ban, and term limits -- the structural reforms that would actually drain the swamp she claims to oppose. In one of the most competitive swing districts in the country, Schrier has mastered the art of being just progressive enough to win without ever threatening the system that keeps her in office. The diagnosis? A chronic case of cautious centrism with no known cure.

Rick Scott is the senior Republican senator from Florida, a man who parlayed a career in the health care industry into a personal fortune and then into political office. Before entering politics, Scott served as CEO of Columbia/HCA, the hospital conglomerate that paid $1.7 billion in what was then the largest Medicare fraud settlement in U.S. history [1]. Scott pleaded the Fifth seventy-five times during the investigation, resigned, and walked away with a $300 million severance package [2]. He was elected governor of Florida in 2010, won a Senate seat in 2018, and briefly served as chairman of the National Republican Senatorial Committee. He now sits on the Commerce, Homeland Security, and Budget committees [3]. It is the kind of biography that makes the phrase "only in America" sound less like a compliment and more like a warning.

On the twenty planks of the 80% Bill, Scott occupies an unusual position: he has endorsed congressional term limits [4] and has championed rhetoric about government accountability, including calls for sunsetting all federal legislation every five years — a proposal so radical it would have put Social Security and Medicare on the chopping block and was disowned by his own party leadership [5]. He has co-sponsored the REEF Act to restrict foreign purchases of U.S. farmland [6] and has spoken favorably about transparency in government spending. Yet on the reforms that would constrain corporate influence — banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, the DISCLOSE Act, banning corporate PACs — Scott has taken no action. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation provisions supported by roughly 90% of Americans [7]. He has opposed paid family leave legislation, shown no interest in right-to-repair measures, and has not supported banning junk fees. On ending forever wars, Scott has voted against repealing the 2001 and 2002 AUMFs [8], keeping Congress's war-making authority firmly delegated to the executive branch.

The carried interest loophole and taxing the ultra-wealthy are especially revealing terrain for Scott. He is himself one of the wealthiest members of the Senate, with an estimated net worth exceeding $200 million [9]. He has consistently opposed wealth taxes and efforts to close the carried interest loophole, positions that happen to benefit him personally. His opposition to Pentagon audits is more puzzling for a self-described fiscal hawk — the Department of Defense has failed its audit every year since 2018, yet Scott has not co-sponsored any legislation to impose consequences for that failure [10]. On the question of banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, Scott has been silent, despite Florida being one of the states hardest hit by institutional investors buying residential properties.

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Scott's campaigns have drawn heavily from the securities and investment industry, health sector, and real estate interests [11]. In his 2024 cycle, his top industry donors included securities and investment firms, insurance companies, and health professionals [11]. He has received substantial contributions from PACs affiliated with the pharmaceutical and insurance industries — the very sectors that benefit most from blocking Medicare drug negotiation and maintaining the health care status quo [12]. His personal wealth, much of it derived from the health care industry he once led into record-setting fraud, means he is legislating on matters in which he has enormous personal financial stakes.

Rick Scott is a senator who speaks the language of populist reform while his record and his bank account tell a very different story. He will champion term limits and rail against Washington insiders, all while being one of the wealthiest insiders in the building. On the issues that matter most to the 80% Bill — stock trading bans, dark money, taxing the ultra-wealthy, Medicare negotiation, corporate PAC bans — he is either absent or actively opposed. His career arc, from Medicare fraud to the Medicare-governing Senate, is less a contradiction than a feature of the system the 80% Bill aims to reform.

Melanie Stansbury won her seat in a 2021 special election after Deb Haaland left to become Secretary of the Interior, carrying New Mexico's 1st district by over 24 points [1]. A former Obama administration staffer and state legislator with a background in science policy and water resource management, Stansbury arrived in Congress as an unabashed progressive — and her voting record has done nothing to complicate that brand. She earned a 96% lifetime score from the League of Conservation Voters [2] and was ranked the 6th most politically left member among House sophomores in GovTrack's 2024 analysis [3].

On the 80% Bill's marquee issues, Stansbury checks a remarkable number of boxes. She co-sponsored the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which includes body camera mandates for federal officers [4]. She joined a bipartisan coalition demanding a floor vote on banning congressional stock trading [5], and she voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which empowered Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices for the first time — a provision she has loudly championed, touting savings for New Mexico seniors on drugs like Eliquis and Jardiance [6]. As Ranking Member on the Oversight Subcommittee on Delivering on Government Efficiency, she sits at the intersection of accountability and spending — relevant turf for anyone who claims to care about Pentagon audits and government transparency [7].

Where Stansbury gets quieter is on the items that lack a natural progressive constituency. Congressional term limits, a policy supported by roughly 87% of Americans, appear nowhere in her cosponsorship record. And while she represents a safely blue district where bold reform carries little electoral risk, she has not put her name on a lifetime lobbying ban for former members of Congress. These are the easy wins that separate the reformers from the partisans.

Follow the money and the picture stays mostly consistent. Her 2024 fundraising leaned on large individual contributions (56%) and PAC money (25%), with JStreetPAC among her top organizational donors at $20,300 [8]. Labor unions and progressive advocacy groups round out her donor base — no oil and gas titans lurking in the fine print. In a district that includes Albuquerque's core, that alignment between donors and votes is the easy part. The harder question is whether Stansbury will use her safe seat to push for the anti-corruption reforms — stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, lobbying restrictions — that don't come with a ready-made PAC check attached.

Dale Whitney Strong arrived in Congress in January 2023 fresh from 27 years on the Madison County Commission, where he rose to become the body's first Republican chairman before trading Huntsville's city hall for Capitol Hill[1][2]. His origin story is the kind that sounds great on a campaign mailer: volunteer firefighter at 13, EMT, 911 dispatcher, son of North Alabama soil who worked his way up from local government[3]. The 5th District he calls home is also home to Redstone Arsenal, NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center, and tens of billions of dollars in annual defense spending — a fact that shapes virtually every decision Strong makes in Washington[4]. Winning his 2024 reelection with 95.4% of the vote in an uncontested race, Strong is the archetypal safe-seat Republican: present for the votes that matter to leadership, absent from anything that might challenge the defense-industrial ecosystem that sustains his district[5].

On the 80% Bill's defining issues, Strong's record is a study in selective populism. He has been a genuine champion of protecting U.S. farmland from foreign adversaries, authoring the bipartisan "Protecting America's Agricultural Land from Foreign Harm Act" with Democratic co-sponsors — a real accomplishment that maps cleanly onto the 80% Bill[6][7]. He voted yes on the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, which he described as protecting elections, though critics note it would erect barriers to registration that fall hardest on poor and minority voters[9].

Where the 80% Bill directly threatens the economic order Strong has built his career serving, his record collapses. He voted against the $60 billion Ukraine aid package in 2024[10]. He voted yes on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law on July 4, 2025 — a bill estimated to cut nearly $1 trillion from Medicaid and drive an estimated 11.8 million people off health coverage, all to finance tax cuts that disproportionately benefit high-income earners[11][12]. On Medicare drug negotiation, Strong co-sponsored legislation to strip $45 billion in IRS enforcement funding from the Inflation Reduction Act, undermining the fiscal architecture that funds drug price reform[13]. Congressional stock trading ban? No public support. DISCLOSE Act? No record of engagement. Term limits? Unaddressed.

Follow the money and the logic becomes cleaner. Strong's top campaign donors include the PACs of Blue Origin, GE Aerospace, Lockheed Martin, and Boeing — the aerospace and defense giants with the deepest footprint at Redstone Arsenal and Marshall Space Flight Center[14][15]. He sits on the House Appropriations Committee in positions spectacularly useful for steering federal contracts toward the contractors who fund his campaigns[17]. In 2025, he championed $4.2 billion for Marshall Space Flight Center and $25 billion for the Golden Dome missile defense program at Redstone Arsenal[18]. Dale Strong will show up — his 1.3% missed vote rate is genuinely admirable[19] — he will pass a meaningful bill on foreign farmland ownership, and he will vote to cut healthcare for nearly twelve million people while collecting checks from aerospace firms that depend on the federal government staying very large indeed. It's a very specific kind of patriotism.

Hillary Scholten's path to Congress runs through an immigration courtroom, a Dutch Reformed church pew, and a fifty-year Republican fortress that she dismantled in a single election cycle. A fourth-generation West Michigander raised in the Christian Reformed Church, Scholten attended Unity Christian High School and Gordon College before spending years as a social worker doing housing advocacy for the AIDS Action Committee and immigration casework for Catholic Charities [1]. She then earned her law degree from the University of Maryland, clerked for the Second Circuit's immigration unit, and joined the Department of Justice through the Attorney General Honors Program, where she worked on immigration and civil rights at the Board of Immigration Appeals from 2013 to 2017 [2]. It is the kind of resume that makes her Republican opponents' attempts to paint her as soft on the border read less like political strategy and more like they did not Google her.

In 2022, Scholten flipped Michigan's 3rd Congressional District — anchored by Grand Rapids, a city so historically Republican that Democrats had not won there in half a century — defeating John Gibbs by thirteen points [3]. She became the first Democratic woman and the first mother to represent the district, a distinction that says as much about the district's history as it does about her candidacy. She won reelection in 2024 with nearly 54 percent of the vote, proving the first victory was not a fluke born of post-Dobbs energy but a genuine realignment in a community where the local Christian university and the local craft brewery now coexist in the same political coalition [4].

Scholten serves on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee and the Small Business Committee, where she holds the ranking member position on the Innovation, Entrepreneurship and Workforce Development Subcommittee [5]. These are not glamorous assignments, but they are the committees where federal dollars actually reach district roads, waterways, and Main Street storefronts — a fact Scholten appears to understand better than colleagues angling for a seat on the panels that generate more cable news appearances and fewer tangible results.

Her most controversial move came in January 2025, when she was one of 46 House Democrats who voted for the Laken Riley Act, which requires DHS to detain undocumented individuals arrested for certain crimes. Progressive critics were incensed, particularly given her immigration law background, but Scholten cited the murder of Ruby Garcia in her own district as the catalyst, noting that her community had been "devastated" by the case [6]. She simultaneously called out the bill's Republican sponsors for "inflammatory, anti-immigrant rhetoric" and continued championing the bipartisan Dignity Act, which would create legal pathways for asylum seekers and undocumented residents already in the country [7]. It is the kind of ideological tightrope walk that makes purists on both sides uncomfortable and pragmatists quietly nod — a fitting summary of a congresswoman who quotes Scripture and cites case law with equal fluency, representing a district that did not know it wanted a Democratic immigration attorney until it got one [8].

Emilia Strong Sykes is a second-term Democrat representing Ohio's 13th district, anchored in Akron and its surrounding communities. A lawyer by training and the daughter of longtime Ohio state legislators Vernon and Barbara Sykes, she arrived in Congress in 2023 after serving as minority leader of the Ohio House — the youngest person and first Black woman to hold that role [1]. She won reelection in 2024 by a razor-thin 51.1%, surviving over $6 million in opposition ad spending, including $3.84 million from the Congressional Leadership Fund [2].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Sykes has been one of the more active Ohio members. She signed a discharge petition to force a House vote on the Restore Trust in Government Act, which would ban stock trading by members of Congress and their families [3]. She introduced the Junk Fee Prevention Act with Rep. Janelle Bynum to crack down on hidden fees in travel, tickets, and utilities [4]. She cosponsored the End Prison Gerrymandering Act to fix Census counting that distorts representation [5], and she introduced the bipartisan GUARD Act with Republican Rob Bresnahan to keep Social Security offices open during government shutdowns [6]. She also voted for the Social Security Fairness Act, which President Biden signed into law [7].

Sykes has not been a lockstep party-line vote. She was one of 46 House Democrats who crossed the aisle to pass the Laken Riley Act, an immigration enforcement bill that drew sharp criticism from progressive groups [8]. She introduced a bipartisan police training bill focused on de-escalation alongside Republicans Brian Fitzpatrick and Don Bacon [9]. In March 2026, she voted to block unauthorized military action in Iran — a position aligned with the 80% Bill's call to repeal outdated war authorizations.

Her donor profile leans on large individual contributions (50%) and PACs (27%), with EMILY's List as a top supporter at $172,546 [2]. She serves on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee and the Science, Space, and Technology Committee — neither of which gives her direct jurisdiction over the heaviest-hitting 80% Bill topics like drug pricing, dark money, or Pentagon audits. On those fronts, her record is largely silent. Still, among Ohio's delegation, Sykes has put more 80% Bill-aligned bills on the board than most — the question is whether she will push on the harder ones that her donors might not love as much.

Rep. Keith Self represents Texas's 3rd congressional district — the affluent suburbs north of Dallas, Collin County country clubs, and the Allen Premium Outlets where, in May 2023, a gunman with an AR-15 killed eight people. Self's response to the massacre in his own district was to tell a CNN interviewer that critics of "thoughts and prayers" are "people that don't believe in an almighty God, who is absolutely in control of our lives." He then advocated arming school staff. This is the man Texas sent to serve on the Foreign Affairs, Veterans' Affairs, and Science committees — a West Point graduate and 25-year Army veteran who apparently believes the Almighty has a plan for mass shootings but not for congressional reform.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Self is a near-total blank. He has taken no public position on banning congressional stock trading, term limits, the DISCLOSE Act, dark money, corporate PAC reform, or any campaign finance measure. His OpenSecrets profile reveals why: his campaign runs on PAC money from Dell Technologies, Chevron, and Raytheon (now RTX), with maximum contributions from Elon Musk and Dallas billionaire Andrew Beal. Nearly two-thirds of his itemized donations come from outside Texas — Virginia, Washington D.C., California — the telltale signature of a congressman funded by the influence industry rather than his own constituents.

On foreign policy, Self chairs the House Foreign Affairs Subcommittee on Europe, where he has taken hawkish positions on Russia while voting against Ukraine aid in April 2024. He called Biden's authorization of ATACMS strikes into Russia "the most dangerous days America has faced since the Cuban Missile Crisis" — the kind of hyperbole that substitutes for a coherent foreign policy. He co-founded the "Sharia Free America Caucus" with Chip Roy in December 2025, a stunt caucus that generated headlines without producing legislation.

On the domestic policies that his constituents in Collin and Hunt counties actually care about — Medicare drug negotiation, taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing tax loopholes, funding Social Security, paid family leave, banning junk fees, right to repair, protecting farmland, banning corporate home buying — Self has done nothing. He is ranked the 2nd most politically right member among House freshmen by GovTrack, which means he votes the party line with mechanical reliability. He joined Chip Roy's crusade against Inflation Reduction Act clean energy tax credits in the "One Big Beautiful Bill" fight, branding them the "green new scam," while his own district's rapidly growing suburbs would benefit directly from those incentives.

Self's military service is genuine and honorable — Airborne Infantry, Special Forces, four continents. But service in uniform does not automatically translate to service in Congress. His constituents support all twenty 80% Bill policies by wide margins. Self supports zero of them. He offers prayers instead of policy, PAC money instead of principle, and culture-war caucuses instead of constituent service. The 3rd district deserves a representative who fights for Collin County, not one who fights for Raytheon's quarterly earnings.
